URL: https://youtu.be/9WCUpqF7GPY?t=55
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: The Lord placed the earth within his sight on the surface of the water and transferred to her his own energy in the form of ability to float on the water. While the enemy stood looking on, Brahma, the creator of the universe, extolled the Lord and the other demigods rained flowers on him. Those who are demons cannot understand how the supreme personality of God had floated the earth on water, but to devotees of the Lord, this is not a very wonderful fact. Not only the earth, but many, many millions of planets are floating in the air and this floating power is endowed upon them by the Lord. There is no other possible explanation. The materialist can explain that the planets are floating by the law of gravitation, but the law of gravitation works under the control of the direction of the Supreme Lord. That is a version of the Bhagavad Gita which confirms by the Lord's statement that behind the material laws or nature's law and behind the growth, maintenance, production and evolution of all the planetary systems, behind everything is the Lord's direction. The Lord's activities could be appreciated only by the demigods headed by Brahma. And therefore when they saw the uncommon prowess of the Lord in keeping the earth on the surface of the water, they showered flowers on him in appreciation of his transcendental activity. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purport.

Are you not ashamed of running away?' 'Are you not ashamed of running away?' And then he uses the word, 'nothing reproachable for shameless creatures.' There are different kinds of demons, atheists, all kinds of impersonalists, mayavadis, who may blaspheme the Lord, or sometimes use words which may be very painful for the Lord, but the Lord sanctions that. The Lord has given us the free will, and He does not interfere. Just like here, at that very moment when Hiranapsha is using these kind of words, he could have just smashed him, killed him, but he allowed him to express whatever he was feeling, even to the extent of abusing the Lord. Unmindful of the Lord, unmindful of all these words which were spoken by Hiranapsha, the Lord, to protect the pious, the devotees, the Lord placed the Earth within the sight of the surface of water, and transferred to her his own energy, in the form of ability to float on the water.

Content: One time, in a particular village, there were some wild boars. Boar we all know, we are talking about the boars here, but Lord in the form of boar is not an animal. So there were wild boars and they would, in the night time, come inside the village and ransack the village, outside the house. If anything is lying, or any food item is lying, they would eat it up, they would scatter things all around, create a big mess, and they'll go away. So everyone was quiet, and they were wild boars, they were also dangerous. By chance, they come at odd time, and somebody is outside, somebody is not slept, even that could be fatal. Fear of life. So they were all very panic-stricken, fearful, and they tried different means to dissuade the boars, to keep them away from the village, but then, they also needed something to eat. They would come at odd times. So they were all fed up, so to say, making different plans, how to keep them away. One fine day, a wise man in that village came to that panchayat. Panchayat is, you know, where all the senior people in the village, they enact policies of what should happen in that village, what should not happen in the village. So he came to that panchayat, who were all discussing among themselves how to keep these wild boars away. So he said, 'I'll give you a simple plan. Just follow what I am saying.' So since they were all very intelligent people, panchayat, so they were a little egoistic also, and, you know, how can someone come with a simple plan which they are not aware of? They were reluctant to accept his advice, but as a last resort, they have tried different things. Okay, let us try this also. So this wise man said, 'you don't want the boars to eat your food, isn't it?' They said, yes. He says, 'let's do the opposite. Let's tell all the villagers to gather a lot of food, each one should bring food from their house, and let's keep the food in the open field. And let the boars eat to their heart's content, whatever they want, let them eat.' So Prima facie was a very foolish advice. On one side, they are trying to safeguard their food, other side, you know, they are saying that, invite the boars to eat the food, and everyone should bring food from their house. Anyway, they had tried different options. As a last resort, let's try this also. So all the villagers were told, they all brought a lot of food items, kept in the center of the field, so to say, and party was organized for the boars to come and enjoy the food. So the smell of the food was very, you know, very nice smell was emanating, and the boars, no time they could sense the food is lying there in the field. An open invitation for them. Initially they were reluctant, apprehensive, who knows it's a trap. Suddenly if you are walking on the road and you find heaps of gold lying there, it's uncommon. So, so the boars were also reluctant and apprehensive, who knows it's a trap, and they were not willing to come. Slowly, one boar came, ate little and suddenly went back, quickly, lest it should not get trapped. The second one came, he also quickly took something and went away. This way, you know, all the boars came and took away. And this phenomena repeated day after day. Slowly what happened to the boars? They got conditioned, and they became very convinced that there is no danger, because every day they are coming, every day they are eating, and every day they are going back. There is no one stopping them. No one is getting trapped. So it is quite, you know, we also build our perceptions. Perception of the world based on our experiences. So they also build a perception that it is great. Lord is all kind, Lord is very merciful. Free food is available, and they would every day come, eat to the heart content, and they will go back. Slowly a time came, they became habituated to free food. They became, so to say, went in the comfort zone. When they are hungry, they would walk into the field, food was lying there, they don't have to worry about anything, eat to the heart content, and go back. So this wise man, then told this panchayat leaders, now start putting poles around the field. And the poles were erected, many, many poles were put. And once you are habituated, you get used to the comfort, then you don't sense the dangers, because you are habituated, it has become your perception that there is no problem here. So they would, in spite of all the poles being erected, they would come every day, and they would go back. Then this wise man said, now start fencing. Put the fence around the poles. So that work also started. Some of the boars saw, but it didn't strike them. Already the perception had built that free food is available for them. And they would go back. A day came, and all the poles were erected all around the field, and the fencing was done, and the boars would come and go away. Then this wise man told, now put a gate. The gate was built. And all the boars passed through the gate, again oblivious of the fact that something is happening in the environment around them. And one fine day, all the boars were in the middle of the field trying to eat, and the wise man had now closed the gate. Fencing all around, the gate is there, and the gate is closed. All the boars are inside the field. All were trapped. This is the case how Maya also traps us.

And weaklings, skinny, you know, seeing them compared to the soldiers in the battlefield who were armed, who were trained. You know. Akshounis, so to say, of soldiers with elephants, with all kinds of horses, well-trained, with all kinds of arms and ammunitions. Compared to that, they were just, you know, ignoble cowards men, weaklings with sticks in their hands. And Arjuna tried to fight against them fiercely so that he can protect the queens because they were attacked. And Arjuna could not protect the queens. He got defeated. Then Arjuna, this is in the Bhagavatam, he makes this wonderful statement. How a devotee can also sometime may get puffed up, may get puffed up, may forget his constitutional position. Because maya is very powerful. But then very soon, the devotee realizes, just like Prabhupada gives an example, marginal energy. The shore of an ocean, sometimes it is under water, sometimes it is, when the waves recede, then it is, there is no water. So likewise, we also get covered by maya, our consciousness. And then we associate with devotees. We associate with devotees. We hear Bhagavatam. We read Prabhupada books. Again, the maya recedes, the covering, so to say, recedes.

Superficial understanding is not to get angry. So Prabhupada says, if there is good reason, you may have to become angry, just to chastise the evil doers and blasphemers. Because we are living in a material world, where everyone is in one sense diseased. Everyone has ego, everyone has pride, everyone is there to put down others. So sometimes, we may have to get angry, to chastise the evil doers, and the blasphemers.

But they consider that these faults can eventually be transformed by certain methods into good qualities. It should be noted that Mahat will never reject or condemn a faulty person, but thinks only how that person can be benefited, [and] can be transformed.

Mahattara also sees good and bad qualities in others, but focuses on and praises the good quality. He gives an example just like a merchant. He is a materialistic person. He is very busy transacting, making money. A typical businessman wants to increase his wealth. Now, a Mahattara sees that a businessman [is] very enthusiastically doing business. Although he may see him as a sense gratifier, ultimately he is materialistic. He wants to make more profit for himself and enjoy the profit. But he notices that he is treating his customers so nicely. And because he is so gentle in his dealings, he is worthy of deliverance. So, in other words, he praises the good quality. Although he sees the fault, he magnifies the good qualities. Then, Vishnu Chakravidh Thakur, even higher than Mahattara is Mahattama, especially great person. Even greater than that person. A Mahattama. A Mahattama sees whatever good qualities others have, he magnifies them, not seeing the even smallest of faults. He refuses to focus on the faults. He sees good qualities and he magnifies them. If he sees 80% good, 20% fault, he ignores the 20% and the 80%, he magnifies it to 100%. In other words, he focuses only on the good.

Why he is traveling is not irrelevant. There is a purpose for all travel. Nobody travels purposely. Only a madman travels purposely. We are traveling in this world. We are embarked on this journey. This life is like a journey. What is the purpose? So conditioned state means we are completely oblivious of these fundamental realities of life. And we are not even bothered about it. This is the meaning of Maya or illusion. Tamasi ma jyotir gama. We are in tamas, deep darkness. These questions don't even strike us. Forget about seeking answers to these questions. Athato brahma jagat. Now that you have got human form of life, try to inquire, Who am I? What is the purpose of my existence? So, in the Vedic times, people were automatically, because they were guided by the Vedas, because they were guided by well qualified brahmanas, kings who were administering the kingdom based on their guidance, the whole by and large was simple living. People would keep their lives simple, bare necessities. One time it happened, a person from the United States, they came to India and you know, he was looking out for a yogi. And so he was going around asking people where to find a yogi. So finally he was told about a yogi and he went looking for him, found his address, got his house, and then reached his place and rang the bell. The door opened. He was welcomed inside.

Such questions are called apavarga vardhanam. So Prabhupada also says this material existence is called pavarga. And spiritual existence, spiritual life is called apavarga vardhanam. Vardhana means to increase. Apavarga. That which nullifies pavarga.

He says, if we, if we treat them like they have come, they are our friends, they are well-wishers, they are coming and correcting us, they are pointing out what is lacking in us, in our operations, in our procedures, in our processes. And if we take their suggestions seriously and improve, then we can do very well. So same is the case as devotees when we, somebody points out some shortcoming. Rather than justifying, conditional life means we justify. He says, no, it's not true. How can you say that? And then we in turn point your mistake, the other person's mistake. What right do you have to speak like this? Have you seen your, this thing? So, if we are always in the mode of introspecting, correcting ourselves, improving ourselves, improving internally, improving our consciousness, remembering Krishna, then in fact we can be so busy that we have no time to point out others' mistakes. We have no time for fault finding. Many times when we have fault finding, we are very externally focused, we are not internally focused. No doubt as authorities or some as leaders, sometimes it is a, it's a thankless job to point out faults, as a service to the other devotee. But we all like this service very much. Right from the beginning stage, we want to do this service. So if with this spirit, if we live, practice the principles of Krishna consciousness, here it says, when it is transferred to the Supreme Personality of Godhead, then one becomes Krishna conscious and his path for liberation opens up. So let's stop here.
Themes: renunciation, devotional service, spiritual progress
 
URL: https://youtu.be/2nrpz-6vu0Q?t=90
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: As soon as he takes his birth in the world, a person who has so many responsibilities and obligations to the public, to the demigods, to the great sages, to the general living beings, to his parents, to his family forefathers, and to many others, when he gives up all such obligations for the sake of the service of the Supreme Lord, he is not punished for such renunciation of obligation. But if for sense gratification a person renounces all such obligations, he is punished by the law of God. So here it is said, such a sadhu engages in staunch devotional service to the Lord without deviation. Now, which sadhu is being referred to? The previous verse talks about what are the symptoms of a sadhu. The symptoms of a sadhu are that he is tolerant, merciful, and friendly to all living entities. He has no enemies, he is peaceful, he abides by the scriptures, and all his characteristics are sublime. So what does this sadhu do with such symptoms? For the sake of the service of the Lord, he renounces all other connections, such as family relationships and friendly acquaintances within the world. Actually, to genuinely progress in spiritual life, it is very important that we cultivate spiritual knowledge and renunciation.

Prabhupada is right here, that when a person takes birth, a person has so many responsibilities and obligations: devarshi, to the devas, to the rishis, the sages; bhutapta, to the public, to the general living entities; ninanam, to the parents, to the family members, the forefathers. But it is said that if one, ya sarvatma sharanam sharanyam, if one surrenders to Mukunda, who is worthy of surrender, who is our supreme father, who is our eternal father, then although one might not be able to fulfill those obligations, Prabhupada writes in the book, he is not punished for such renunciations. That's why many times when devotees join, it is very difficult for the family members, his friends, other members to understand how he can be so callous, how he can be so reckless. Sometimes he may be the only son in the family, and it is beyond their comprehension. It defies all logic, all rational. And that's why people even use the word, he's been brainwashed, because from their perspective, it doesn't make any sense. A child who has been raised by the parents, they've invested love, affection, money, got him educated. Now is the time when he should be taking care of them, staying with them. And he says, no, Krishna is supreme and I want to serve him, even at the cost of not serving them. So many times devotees take this bold step.

The opportunity of meeting death for the sake of the country is much higher, although death can come anywhere, anytime, to anyone. But it is dangerous, because day in and day out you are exposed to an environment where casualties can happen. Now a person knowing that very well, a person agrees or rather could be, somebody may say it's a job, after all it's a livelihood. And such people willingly go and take all risk to protect their motherland, to protect their country, to fight for their nation. Although they may have back home, young wife, young children, maybe only one daughter, but the person is willing to take the risk for the sake of the country. And if a person gets killed, nobody says that he was callous. Nobody says that he was reckless, he left his family. He should have taken care of the family because people understand that fighting for the country is a higher cause. In fact, they say that he is a martyr. He sacrificed his life. He celebrated. His funeral happens with military honors. People salute him for his sacrifice. So here we see an example of the interest of the nation is higher than the interest of the family, and a person who, for the sake of country's interest, sacrificed the interest of the family, of his children, he is respected. That's why in Varanashrama system, even though everyone was doing their respective duties, may not be directly engaged in devotional service. Avaishyas may be just doing their business, or workers may just be doing some work, but because the overall society was guided by the Brahmanas, everyone, by doing their respective duties, prescribed duties, was moving gradually in the right direction, and that is towards the ultimate goal of life, which is to surrender to Krishna, God-realization or self-realization. So that's why, you see, this renunciation was celebrated in Vedic times. Unfortunately, because this knowledge is not prevalent in present-day society, these concepts are not understood by people in general. Therefore, renunciation does not have much value. Content: So we should remember our good fortune. So here it is said that when one engages in these nine devotional activities, so as devotees, our endeavor should be, every moment should be engaged in these nine activities, one of these nine activities. Either we are hearing, or we are chanting, or we are remembering, or we are serving, or we are praying. And when one does that, he will be free from the influence of the modes of material nature. Modes of material nature is like an imprisonment. Yesterday we talked about the bodily concept of life is an imprisonment. The modes of material nature force us to get into, come down from the spiritual platform to the material platform. When one starts thinking in terms of I and mine, body and the associated bodily relatives. One time, Shankaracharya, he was looking for a key outside his house, in the street, underneath a street light. He had lost the key. And he was desperately trying to find out where. And one of his disciples came back to the ashram and saw Shankaracharya himself, kneeling and looking for the key, underneath the street light. So naturally, he also said, let me also, he came to know that he has lost the key. So he also started searching for the key. And it went on for some time, 10 minutes, 15 minutes. And then the disciple asked Shankaracharya, do you remember? Do you remember where you lost the key? You know, it is natural when we, this question comes in mind. He says, you lost the key inside, near the bed. And why are you searching here? So he said, inside, because the oil got over in the lamp, it became very dark. So I thought, let me search the key outside, where there is light. So the disciple was really, how can, I mean, he didn't say, but it was apparent from his looks. How can he be so foolish? Lost the key inside and is looking for the key outside, in the light. So Shankaracharya just smiled at, when the disciple was wonderstruck with this answer. So Shankaracharya smiled. And he said, isn't this the case with all of us? This is a very important lesson. Actually, living entity is intrinsically happy. Sat, chit, and ananda. He doesn't need anything else. He is amsa of the Lord, in association with the Lord. He is completely happy. Joyful. Experiencing blissful relationship, reciprocal relationship with the Lord. That is the quality of the jiva. Actually, all of us, we are not happy with the jiva. We are looking for happiness in the external world through sense gratification and so many other elaborate arrangements for that happiness. Hoping against hope that I will be happy by this arrangement, by that arrangement, by this achievement, by this accomplishment. And that's why the goalposts keep changing. That's why nobody is happy. Krishna in the Gita says, third chapter, when one is self-realized, when he is illumined from within, when he is self-satisfied, he is happy within, he has no duty to perform.

Prayers, yes, the prayers what we have, you know, sung by acharyas, but we can also speak to the deities. We can talk just like you talk to somebody else. Devotees express their mind before the deities and in many instances the deities also give answers. So we should also, Prabhupada says, the deity, although we know it's made of stone, he is not stone. He is stone to the stone-hearted. So when we chant, when we read, when we hear, what we are doing is our heart which is stone-hearted right now. We have no devotion. We have no love for Krishna. We have no attraction for Krishna. We have attraction for all this material illusory energy, the variegatedness connected by maya. If one is stone-hearted then Krishna will stand like stone forever. So why do we do this sadhana? Why do we do this chanting and all that? Slowly this heart which is stone-hearted, there is a transformation happening. For a stone-hearted the deity will be like stone. It will stand like a stone forever. But if one is soft-hearted by chanting Hare Krishna mantra, why we chant? The heart is becoming soft. Then he will talk with Krishna. That is the difference.

For example, the duration of life on higher planets is far, far greater than on this planet. It is said that on Moon the duration of life is such that our six months are equal to one day. We cannot even imagine the duration of life on the highest planet. It is stated in the Bhagavad Gita that Brahma's twelve hours are inconceivable even to our mathematicians. These are all descriptions of the external energy of the Lord, or Maya. Besides these, there are other opulences which the yogis can achieve by their mystic power. They are all material. A devotee does not aspire for all these material pleasures, although they are available to him simply by wishing. By the grace of the Lord, a devotee can achieve wonderful success simply by willing, but a real devotee does not like that. Lord Chaitanya Mahaprabhu has taught that one should not desire material opulence or material reputation, nor should one try to enjoy material beauty. One should simply aspire to be absorbed in the transcendental service of the Lord, even if one does not get liberation but has to continue the process of birth and death, or even if one does not have the opportunity to do so. Actually, however, to one who engages in Krishna consciousness, liberation is already guaranteed. Devotees enjoy all the benefits of the higher planets and the Vaikuntha planets also. It is especially mentioned here, Bhagavatim Bhadram. In the Vaikuntha planets, everything is eternally peaceful, yet a pure devotee does not even aspire to be promoted there. But still he gets the advantage. He enjoys the benefits of the higher planets, and the higher planets also. He enjoys all the facilities of the material and spiritual world, even during the present lifespan. Thus, because he is completely absorbed in the thought of Me, the devotee does not desire even the highest benediction obtainable in the upper planetary system, including Satyaloka.

If we are helping that, I am the well-wisher, I am really the one who can help him, that's a wrong understanding. We may even help a person, but with the understanding that, he is amsa of the Lord, I am amsa of the Lord. On behalf of the Lord, I am helping him.

People are perplexed. So one of the definitions or understanding of God is He is anadi. He is not an ordinary mundane person. Anadir adir govinda sarvakarana karanam. He is the cause of all causes. Content: Although He is without beginning, He is the beginning of everyone. So then further Prabhupada mentions that, although the Lord is a person, He is present everywhere. He is perceivable everywhere, because He is self-effulgent. This is again, very difficult for many people to comprehend, because all along we have an understanding of a person who is limited in time and space. If you see a person in a particular place, you can very confidently conclude that He is not in another place. But here it is said that the Lord is all-pervading. If He is a person, how can He be all-pervading? Because all persons whom we have seen are limited by time and space. If they are present in place A, they cannot be simultaneously present in place B. So here it is said that the Lord is present everywhere by His energies. The entire creation is pervaded by the Lord. Nothing but the Lord and His energies. Energies, broadly speaking, material energy and spiritual energy. The Lord is present anywhere and everywhere, and He is present through the distribution of energy. In the Bhagavad Gita also it is confirmed that the Lord is all-pervading and exists everywhere by two kinds of energy, one material and other spiritual. Both material and spiritual energies are spread everywhere and that is a proof of existence of the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

Actually, if you go to a printer, all these colorful prints, all these beautiful calendars, all colorful posters, they are just made of... anyone has an idea how many colors? Four colors: C, M, Y, K. Cyan, magenta, black and yellow. If we closely see the... that's why we call it DPI, dots per inch. Higher the DPI, you will not be able to see the dots. It's so close, so densely packed. And different colors are produced by the different size of dots and how densely they are close to each other. If you see a picture which is by and large reddish in color, you'll have more of magenta dots and densely populated. If you see Radha Krishna calendar and if you see dress of Radharani, it's yellow in color. If you closely see with a very powerful lens, you will see it's made of yellow dots. And if you see orange, it is combination of yellow and magenta dots. And human eye cannot perceive it. So just like we have unlimited shades possible by combination of three colors, four colors, likewise unlimited combinations of behaviors, perspective of life, consciousness is possible by the influence of these three modes of material nature.

We'll have a particular kind of behavior. In general, we will be lazy. We will be clouded with different kinds of ignorance. As we rise in the modes, in rajo guna, in tamo guna, sorry, in sattva guna, our perspective changes. That's why a symptom of sattva guna is enlightenment, is knowledge. Just like an example is given, if you take an alkali, we have all studied in chemistry, alkali and acid. If you take an alkali and you combine with another alkali, there is no reaction. You take a powerful acid and combine with another acid, there is no reaction. You take an alkali and an acid, you mix together, there is a powerful reaction. Likewise, a person who is influenced by tamo guna, if he goes to a tamasic place, will he feel out of place? No. He is comfortable. There is no reaction. If you take a person who is predominantly in sattva guna, you take him to a place where tamo guna is predominant, he will feel very uncomfortable. He will feel like, let me just get away from this place as early as possible. See, different behaviors are all nothing but the combination of modes which are influencing us.

Themes: material nature, spiritual nature, impregnation
 
URL: https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=30
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: This impregnation of material nature is described in the Bhagavad Gita, 14th chapter, verse 3. Material nature's primal factor is the mahatattva, or breeding source of all varieties. This part of material nature, which is called pradhāna, as well as brahman, is impregnated by the supreme personative godhead and delivers varieties of living entities. Material nature in this connection is called brahman because it is a perverted reflection of the spiritual nature. It is described in the Vishnu Purāṇa that the living entities belong to the spiritual nature. The potency of the supreme lord is spiritual and the living entities, although they are called marginal potency, they are also spiritual. If the living entities were not spiritual, this description of impregnation by the supreme lord would not be applicable. The supreme lord does not put a semen into that which is not spiritual, but it is stated here that the supreme lord puts a semen into material nature. This means that the living entities are spiritual by nature. After impregnation, material nature delivers all kinds of living entities, beginning from the greatest living creature, Lord Brahma, down to the insignificant ant, in all varieties of form. In the Bhagavad Gita, 14.4, material nature is clearly mentioned as sarvayoni. This means that all varieties of species, demigods, human beings, animals, birds and beasts, whatever is manifested, material nature is the mother, and the supreme personality of Godhead is the seed-giving father. Generally it is experienced that the father gives life to the child, but the mother gives its body. Although the seed of life is given by the father, the body develops within the womb of the mother. Similarly, the spiritual living entities are impregnated into the womb of material nature, but the body being supplied by the material nature takes on many different species and forms of life. The theory that the symptoms of life are manifested by the interaction of the 24 material elements is not supported here. The living force comes directly from the supreme personality of Godhead and is completely spiritual. Therefore, no material scientific advancement can produce life. The living force comes from the spiritual world and this has nothing to do with the interaction of material elements.
Themes: material nature, Krishna's direction, transcendental knowledge
 
URL: https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=177
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
 
Content: So in the Gita, Krishna talks about material nature. Generally people understand this whole creation, what we see around us: beautiful landscapes, beautiful flowers, mountains, lakes. They appreciate nature's beauty, gifts of nature, nature which means prakriti, nature's bounty. Krishna in the Gita says this material nature, which is producing so many moving and non-moving beings, is working under the direction of Krishna. Mayadhyakshena prakriti suyate sa chara acharam. Chara means moving, acharam means non-moving. This world is designed in such a way that if someone wants to defy God, deny God, don't want to see the hand of the Lord, he can still somehow explain how we came into being because of our parents, how a flower and fruit is coming into being because of the seed, how the seed came into being because of some fruit earlier. So what, if we don't have shastra chakshu, to some extent we can see cause and effect relationship between various phenomena what we see in this material world. But with shastra chakshu, with transcendental knowledge which Krishna shares with us in the Gita, we can see beyond what meets our eyes. That's why it's called transcendental knowledge which transcends the knowledge which we can assimilate through our material senses.

URL: https://youtu.be/C52Kr8wwiIo?t=330

Content: I'm sure all of you have heard about this example. Some of us have stayed in the hostel during our college days. Even in my time when I was in college, either we'll receive money through a check or through a money order. So in money order system, you deposit the money in a particular post office, you fill a form and the post office, wherever you have to send money, receives the money and then the postman goes and delivers the money to the person whosoever is mentioned in the form. So if we see why a person who is staying in the hostel, he is getting the money, the money with which he can pay his mess bills, sustain himself, buy various other items, what he needs for his sustenance, he can see a direct correlation that it's the postman who is coming and delivering the money. And using that money, he is paying the mess bill, he is able to buy things, he is able to buy some fruits. So in other words, there's a direct correlation between the postman and my ability to pay the bills. So superficially speaking that we have to be grateful to that postman because he is the one, unless he gives me the money, how will I pay the bills? How will I survive? So I have to be grateful to him. Someone who cannot see beyond this will say that the postman is maintaining me because the direct correlation, unless he gives you the money every month, you cannot sustain. But with little intelligence, we know the postman, although he is giving the money physically, he is just being an instrument. It is his duty to deliver that money. But there is someone else who is depositing the money in the post office because of which the postman is getting the instruction to go and deliver this money. So if we take a step back and introspect, what's the cause of the postman giving the money? It's the father who has deposited that money in the post office.

Every one of us have different abilities, skills. Some are good learners. Some have good intelligence. Some are buddhu. Some are able to remember things quite well. So Krishna says the ability is me. Sometimes the thoughts are empowering thoughts, good thoughts, sometimes the thoughts may be disempowering, maybe bad thoughts. Abominable thoughts. The source of such thoughts, good or bad, is definitely the mind, but mind is an instrument. We have a gross body and a subtle body. Mind is part of the subtle body. Ultimately it is the soul. Even mind is a material element. It's the consciousness. And if we are in material consciousness or material conception of life, mind will generate one kind of thoughts, one kind of desires. Here it is said, from this evolves the mind whose thoughts and reflections give rise to desire. So if our consciousness is impure, if we are in material consciousness, if we have material conception of life, if we are in bodily concept of life, then we will have, the mind will generate a particular kind of thoughts and desires. We all know thoughts are very powerful thing, but what is the cause, what generates a particular kind of thoughts? It's our consciousness. Ultimately mind is also a matter, material element, although it is subtle. And if we are in a spiritual conception of life, if we are, have spiritual consciousness or Krishna consciousness, then naturally our desires will be spiritual in nature, empowering in nature, our thoughts will be pure.

Themes: pure devotion, material desire, Krishna consciousness
 
URL: https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=90
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: In material existence, when a devotee is not completely purified, he expects material benefit from the Lord in the form of relief from material miseries, or he wants material gain, advancement in knowledge of the relationship between the Supreme Personality of Godhead and the living entity, or knowledge as to the real nature of the Supreme Lord. When a person is transcendental to these conditions, he is called a pure devotee. He does not engage himself in the service of the Lord for any material benefit or for understanding of the Supreme Lord. His only interest is that he loves that Supreme Personality of Godhead and he spontaneously engages in satisfying Him. The highest example of pure devotional service is that of the Gopis in Vrindavan. They are not interested in understanding Krishna, but only in loving Him. That platform of love is the pure state of devotional service. Unless one is advanced to this pure state of devotional service, there is a tendency to desire elevation to a higher material position. A mixed devotee may desire to be a pure devotee, or he may desire to enjoy a comfortable life on another planet with a greater span of life, such as on Ramaloka. These are material desires, but because a mixed devotee engages in the service of the Lord, ultimately, after many, many lives of material enjoyment, he undoubtedly develops Krishna consciousness, and the symptom of this Krishna consciousness is that he is no longer interested in any part or any sort of material elevated life. He does not even aspire to be a pure devotee. He does not even aspire to become a personality like Lord Brahma. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Bhava.
Themes: living entity, qualitative oneness, quantitative difference
 
URL: https://youtu.be/BWV5fmmOZaQ?t=185
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna mentions that the living entities, the spirit soul, the Atma, is his Amsa. mam eva amsa jiva loke jiva bhuta sanatana. And Prabhupada explains that how, as Amsa of the Lord, we are qualitatively the same as that of the Lord. Prabhupada gives an example of how a drop of water in the sea has the same characteristics as that of the entire sea, or the entire ocean. But quantitatively, we are different. A drop of water is very minuscule compared to the entire ocean. So, being Amsa of the Lord, qualitatively we are one with the Lord and we also are Sat, Chit, and Ananda. Sat means eternal, Chit means full of knowledge, and Ananda, blissful. And these Amsas of the Lord, in the spiritual sense, are the same as the Amsa of the Lord.

In the material existence, when a devotee is not completely purified, he expects material benefit from the Lord. When we are serving, we are expecting some benefit, peace of mind, satisfaction, feeling good. In the form of relief from material miseries or he wants material gain, lot of people are kamabhaktas. They are serving the Lord because they have some desires to be fulfilled. Advancement in knowledge of relationship between the Supreme Personality of Godhead and living and some are serving the Lord or engaged in devotional service to get enlightened, to know their jigyasu, they want to know. When a person is now, this is all mixed devotee, he wants knowledge, he wants relief from miseries, he wants some happiness, he wants pleasure for the self. Lord is a means. Service to the Lord is a mean. End is what? My pleasure, my satisfaction, My enlightenment, My liberation, My going back to Godhead. That is mixed devotee. So, Prabhupada says, when a person is transcendental to these conditions, he is called a pure devotee. Pure devotee does not engage in devotional service to the Lord because he wants knowledge, because he wants to get enlightened, because he wants pleasure. he wants Satchitananda.

And the symptom of this Krishna consciousness is that he is no longer interested in any sort of materially elevated life. Right now we have desire to be comfortable, to be peaceful, to be properly situated, to be happy. So slowly all those anarthas, all those impure desires, all the material desires, slowly starts getting wiped out. And pure desires to love Krishna, to serve Krishna, to blissfully engage in the service of Krishna, manifest. And that is what we are trying to achieve. It may happen in one life, it may happen many, many lifetimes. Depends on how sincere we follow the process. We'll stop here.

URL: https://youtu.be/eUMUuoU-WE4?t=68
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: Perception of the disagreeable arises from attachment. A devotee has no personal attachment to anything. Therefore, for him, there is no question of agreeable or disagreeable. For the service of the Lord, he can accept anything, even though it may be disagreeable to his personal interest. In fact, he is completely free from personal interest, and thus anything agreeable to the Lord is agreeable to him. For example, for Arjuna, at first, fighting was not agreeable. To the Lord, he accepted the fighting as agreeable. That is a position of a pure devotee. For his personal interest, there is nothing which is agreeable or disagreeable. Everything is done for the Lord, and therefore he is free from attachment and detachment. That is a transcendental stage of neutrality. A pure devotee enjoys life in the pleasure of the Supreme Lord. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta because of his transcendental intelligence. A pure devotee is equipoised in his vision and sees himself to be uncontaminated by matter.

And he says, it is not possible that all three statements are true simultaneously. One of them has to be wrong.

URL: https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1099
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: So what happens when we are dabbling in sense enjoyment? When there is a drive force enjoying the senses, that our ācāryas say is called kūṭam, that drive to enjoy. Arjuna also asked Kṛṣṇa, why is it, Kṛṣṇa, that sometimes we are forced to act in sinful ways much against our wish? Even we realize in our day-to-day life, on one side we want to get up early in the morning, the other side, when we get up in the morning, when the alarm rings, there is another force which says, no, let me sleep. So Kṛṣṇa was asked, what is that force? Why is it that many times we act in sinful ways much against our wish, as if there is some kind of a push? So that push is called kūṭam, and that kūṭam results in actions, we act based on that force, and that actions deposit the reaction in our system, and that reactions are deposited in the form of bījam, the seed, bījam. And just like in a seed, we see different seed sprouts at different times, some seeds sprout in one day, some seed sprouts in four days. So likewise, this bījam fructifies into reaction, which manifests in the life as enjoyment or suffering, which is called a stage of falon mukham, falā, falā of the actions which we have done, and that stage is called falon mukham.
Themes: karma, avidyā, lust
 
URL: https://youtu.be/M1HwvfEEzys?t=1228
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: So what we are seeing, different people enjoying and suffering differently, is what has started manifesting in their life. Sometimes people get bewildered when they see good people suffering or bad people enjoying life. That's because, even though at present they are doing good, but there are a lot of bījams which are stored from the actions done in the past, which are now fructifying. A person may have done good in the past, as a result there are good bījams stored, but currently he may be doing bad things, but still he may be enjoying the reactions of the past deeds. And vice versa, for a person who is doing good now, could be result of, still could be suffering because of the actions done in the past. Now ācāryas have even gone further, just like in any other field of activity, they do root cause analysis. What is the cause? What is the cause? What is the cause? So ācāryas have analyzed kūtam. Kūtam is that which forces us to act in sinful ways. Sinful ways or whatever, forces us to act. So they have analyzed what is a step behind kūtam. That force is called kāma, lust. Here it says desire to enjoy the senses. What is lust? Desire to enjoy independent of Kṛṣṇa. And they have gone one step ahead and realized what is the cause of that lust, desire, which results in kūtam, which results in bījam, which results in falonmukham. And they found out that the result of that lust, or kāma, is avidyā. In Bhāgavatam it is mentioned, avidyā kāma karma bhī.

There are so many things which we can do, prāyaścitta, whereby we can take care of the reactions. By doing some yajñas, by doing some homas, we can nullify those reactions. It doesn't create any good or bad reactions. It destroys avidhyā, destroys kāma, cleans up our consciousness.

Next day you do all your convincing, and nobody will buy a book. Then you realise, my God, it's a fact, I am not the convincer. This happens over a period of time. And while we are engaged in devotional service, slowly, dhire dhire, dhire dhire, that purification happens. One comes to our senses. Unfortunately, to some extent, you can fast track the process, otherwise it will take time. That's why it's a dhairiya, patience. You start with polluted consciousness, as we engage, the purification of consciousness is inbuilt in the system. For that, what we have to do, we have to, we have to, the process, shravanam, hearing, shunvata, swakataha, krishna, punya, shravana, kirtana, nashta, praesho, badreshu, nityam, bhagavata, sevaya, when we do that, all this abhadras, dirty things, which is clinging to us, clinging to our consciousness, which has overpowered our consciousness, completely gets subsided. So, we have to, just like diamond, it's actually a very precious stone, but when you see original, this thing, it is unpolished, uncut, it is not shiny, but because of that polish, because of rubbing, because of different treatment, it goes through, it starts shining. Same is the case, what happens with us. Okay? Stop here.
Themes: supreme absolute truth, energy of the Lord, Vaishnava philosophy
 
URL: https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=50
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: The supreme absolute truth, the Personality of Godhead, is one, and He is spread everywhere by His impersonal feature. This is clearly expressed in the Bhagavad-Gita. Lord Krishna says everything that is experienced is but an expansion of my energy. Everything is sustained by Him, but that does not mean that He is in everything. Sense perceptions, such as oral perception of the sound of a drum, visual perception of a beautiful woman, or perception of a delicious taste of a milk preparation by the tongue, all come through different senses and are therefore differently understood. Therefore, sensory knowledge is divided in different categories, although actually everything is one as a manifestation of the energy of the Supreme Lord. Similarly, the energies of fire are heat and illumination, and by these two energies, fire can display itself in many varieties or in diversified sense perception. Mayavadi philosophers declared the diversity felt that it was false, but Vaishnava philosophers do not accept the different manifestations as false. They accept them as non-different from the Supreme Personality of Godhead because they are a display of his diverse energies. The philosophy that the absolute is true and this creation is false, Brahma Satyam Jagam Idhya, is not accepted by Vaishnava philosophers. The example is given that although all that glitters is not gold, this does not mean that a glittering object is false. For example, an oyster shell appears to be golden. This appearance of golden hue is due to the perception of the eyes, but that does not mean that the oyster shell is false. Similarly, by seeing the form of Lord Krishna, one cannot understand what He actually is.
Themes: knowledge, form of Krishna, sense perception
 
URL: https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=177
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: This does not mean that He is false. The form of Krishna has to be understood as it is described in the books of knowledge such as Brahma Samhita. Ishwara Parama Krishna Satchitananda Vigraha. Krishna, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, has an eternal, blissful, spiritual body. By our imperfect sense perception, we cannot understand the form of the Lord. We have to acquire knowledge about Him. Therefore it is said here, Gyanam Ekam. Bhagavad Gita confirms that there are fools who, simply upon seeing Krishna, consider Him a common man. They do not know the unlimited knowledge, power, and opulence of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Material sense speculation leads to the conclusion that the Supreme is formless. It is because of such mental speculation that the conditioned soul remains in ignorance under the spell of illusory energy. The Supreme Person has to be understood by the transcendental sound vibrated by Him in the Bhagavad Gita, wherein He says that He is nothing superior to Himself. The impersonal Brahman effulgence is resting on His personality. The purified absolute vision of the Bhagavad Gita is compared to the true and absolute consciousness of the Supreme, to the river Ganges. Ganges water is so pure that it can purify even the asses and cows. But anyone who, disregarding the pure Ganges, wishes to be purified instead by the filthy water flowing in a drain cannot be successful. Similarly, one can successfully attain pure knowledge of the Absolute only by hearing from the pure Absolute Himself. In this verse, it is clearly said.
Themes: knowledge, consciousness, scripture
 
URL: https://youtu.be/34UwCtltuAQ?t=275
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: That those who are averse to the Supreme Personality of Godhead speculate with their imperfect senses about the nature of the Absolute Truth. The formless Brahman conception, however, can be received only by oral reception and not by personal experience. Knowledge is therefore acquired by oral reception. It is confirmed in the Vedanta Sutra, Shastra Yonit Tvaat. One has to acquire pure knowledge from the authorized scriptures. So-called speculative arguments about the Absolute Truth are therefore useless. The actual identity of the living entity is his consciousness, which is always present while the living entity is awake, dreaming, or in deep sleep. Even in deep sleep, he can perceive by consciousness whether he is happy or distressed. Thus, when consciousness is displayed through the medium of subtle and gross material bodies, it is covered. But when the consciousness is purified in Krishna consciousness, one becomes free from the entanglement of repeated birth and death. When uncontaminated pure knowledge is unencumbered from the modes of material nature, the actual identity of the living entity is discovered. He is eternally a servitor of the Supreme Personality of Godhead. The process of uncovering is like this. The rays of sunshine are luminous, and the sun itself is also luminous. In the presence of the sun, the rays illuminate just like the sun. But when the sunshine is covered by the spell of a cloud, or maya, then darkness, the imperfection, or of perception, begins. Therefore, to get out of the entanglement of the spell of nascence, one has to awaken his spiritual consciousness or Krishna consciousness in terms of authorized scriptures. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Papad.

URL: https://youtu.be/Ng51E26XRgU?t=138
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: When we see milk, we see that it is white. When we taste it, it appears that the milk is very palatable. When we touch milk, it appears very cold. When we smell milk, it appears to be very good flavor. And when we hear, we understand it is called milk. Perceiving milk with different senses, we say that it is something white, something very delicious, something very aromatic, and so on. Actually, it is milk. Similarly, those who are trying to find the supreme godhead by mental speculation may approach the bodily effulgence, or the impersonal Brahman. And those who are trying to find the supreme godhead by yoga practice may find him as localized super soul. But those who are directly trying to approach the supreme truth by practice of bhakti yoga can see him face to face as the supreme person. Ultimately, the supreme person is the destination of all different processes. The fortunate person who, by following the principles of scriptures, becomes completely purified of all material contamination, surrenders unto the supreme lord as everything. Just as one can appreciate the real taste of milk with the tongue, and not with eyes, nostrils, or ears, one can similarly appreciate the supreme truth perfectly and with all relishable pleasure, only through one path, devotional service. This is also confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita, bhakti avam abhijanati. If one wants to understand the absolute truth in perfection, he must take to devotional service. Of course, no one can understand the truth in perfection, that is not possible for the infinitesimal living entities, but the highest point of understanding by the living entity is reached by discharge of devotional service, not otherwise.
Themes: brahmananda, transcendence, devotional service
 
URL: https://youtu.be/Ng51E26XRgU?t=240
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: By following various scriptural paths, one may come to impersonal effulgence of the supreme personality of Godhead. The transmittal pleasure derived from merging with or understanding the impersonal Brahman is very extensive because Brahman is ananta, tad brahma, nishkalam anantam. Brahmananda is unlimited, but the unlimited pleasure can also be surpassed. That is the nature of the transcendence. The unlimited can be surpassed also, and that higher platform is Krishna. When one deals directly with Krishna, the mellow and the humor relished by reciprocation of devotional service is incomparable, even with the pleasure derived from transcendental Brahman. Prabodhananda Saraswati therefore says that kaivalya, the Brahman pleasure, is undoubtedly very great and is appreciated by many philosophers. But to a devotee who has understood how to derive pleasure from exchanging devotional service with the Supreme Lord, this unlimited Brahman appears to be hellish. One should try, therefore, to transcend even the Brahman pleasure in order to approach the position of dealing with Krishna face to face.

URL: https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=46
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: The word Drishi Adi Bhi is significant. According to Jiva Goswami, Drishi means Gyana, philosophical research. By different processes of philosophical research under different concepts, such as the process of Gyana Yoga, the same Bhagavan or the Supreme Personality of Godhead is understood as impersonal Brahman. Similarly, by the eightfold yoga system, he appears as the Paramatma. But in pure Krishna consciousness or knowledge in purity, when one tries to understand the supreme absolute truth, one realizes him as the supreme person. The transcendence is realized simply on the basis of knowledge. The words used here, Paramatma, Eshwara, Puman are all transcendental and they refer to Supersoul. Supersoul is also described as Purusha, but the word Bhagavan directly refers to the Supreme Personality of Godhead who is full of opulence, six opulence, wealth, fame, strength, beauty, knowledge, and renunciation. He is the personality of Godhead in different spiritual skies. The various descriptions of Paramatma, Eshwara, and Puman indicate that the expansions of the Supreme Personality of Godhead are unlimited. Ultimately, to understand the Supreme Personality of Godhead, one has to accept Bhakti Yoga. By executing Gyana Yoga or Dhyana Yoga, one has to eventually approach the Bhakti Yoga platform, and then, Paramatma, Eshwara, Puman, etc. are all clearly understood.
Themes: devotional service, liberation, knowledge
 
URL: https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=165
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: It is recommended in the Second Chant of Srimad Bhagavatam that whether one is a devotee or a fruitive actor or a liberationist, if he is intelligent enough, he should engage himself with all seriousness in the process of devotional service. It is also explained that whatever one desires, which is obtainable by fruitive activities, even if one wants to be elevated to higher planets, can be achieved simply by execution of Dhyana Yoga, or devotional service. Since the Supreme Lord is full in six opulences, he can bestow any one of them upon the worshipper. The one Supreme Personality of Godhead reveals himself to different thinkers as the Supreme Person or Impersonal Brahman or Paramatma. Impersonalists merge into the Impersonal Brahman, but that is not achieved by worshipping the Impersonal Brahman. If one takes to devotional service, and at the same time desires to merge into the existence of Supreme Lord, he can achieve that. If someone desires at all to merge into the existence of Supreme, he has to execute devotional service. The devotee can see the Supreme Lord face to face, but the Jnani, the Empiric Philosopher or Yogi cannot. They cannot be elevated to the position of associates of the Lord. There is no evidence in the scriptures stating that by cultivating knowledge or by worshipping the Impersonal Brahman, one can become a personal associate of the Supreme Personality. Nor by executing the yogic principles can one become an associate of the Supreme Godhead. Only for the devotees is the Lord visible.
Themes: Supreme Personality of Godhead, impersonal Brahman, Paramatma
 
URL: https://youtu.be/2u7wj9NXkbk?t=277
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: Here the statement, Drishi Adi B is significant. Since the Supreme Personality of Godhead is both invisible and visible, there are different features of the Lord. The Paramatma feature and the Brahman feature are invisible, but Bhagavan feature is visible. In the Vishnu Purana this fact is very nicely explained. The universal form of the Lord and the formless Brahman effulgence of the Lord being invisible are inferior features. The concept of the universal form is material, and the concept of impersonal Brahman is spiritual. But the highest spiritual understanding is the personality of Godhead. The Vishnu Purana states, vishnu brahma svarupena svayam eva vyastitihi. Brahman's real feature is Vishnu or the Supreme Brahman is Vishnu, svayam eva that is his personal feature. The supreme spiritual conception is the Supreme Personality of Godhead. It is confirmed in the Bhagavad Gita, yad gatva na nivartante tad dhamam paramam mama. That specific abode called paramam mama is the place from which once one attains it, one does not return to the miserable conditional life. Every place, every space and everything belongs to Vishnu. But when he personally lives in tad dhamam paramam, his supreme abode, one has to make one's destination the supreme abode of the Lord. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Purport.

Themes: Krishna's nature, Qualitative oneness, Spiritual world
 
URL: https://youtu.be/LyHytEHamtU?t=0
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
 
Content: In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna mentions that the living entities, the spirit soul, the Atma, is his Amsha. Prabhupada explains that as Amsha of the Lord, we are qualitatively the same as that of the Lord. Prabhupada gives the example of how a drop of water in the sea has the same characteristics as the entire sea or the entire ocean, but quantitatively we are different. A drop of water is very minuscule compared to the entire ocean. So being Amsha of the Lord, qualitatively we are one with the Lord and we also are Sat, Chit, and Ananda. Sat means eternal, Chit means full of knowledge, and Ananda, blissful. These Amsha of the Lord in the spiritual world, in the association of the Lord, are blissfully engaged in the service of the Lord. In the spiritual world, the Lord is the center. There is no Aham, there is pure love, and everyone is engaged in serving others. A devotee may engage in serving other devotees, but keeping the Lord in the center. So there the mentality is Pehle Aap, Your service. Your seva enabling others to serve Krishna directly or indirectly, everyone is engaged in serving the Supreme Lord in glorifying the Supreme Lord.

The more you try to indulge, gratify the senses it's like putting ghee in the fire more the fire burns. It aggravates. So therefore the Vedic scriptures say from the very beginning regulate the senses, tapasya. Tapo divyam shudhyet brahma saukhyam tu anantam. Try to regulate the senses. How? By tapo divyam, by divine tapasya, shudhyet which will purify our existence. And what will happen as a result? Brahma saukhyam tu anantam. We will experience real contentment. That's it. Real contentment. Real happiness.

Content: So, Krishna in the Gita says, bhoktaram yajnatapasam sarvaloka maheshwaram suhridam sarvabhutanam nyatvamam shantim rachati bhoktaram yajnatapasam. Bhokta means, I am the enjoyer. I am the real enjoyer. This reminds me of a story of the limbs going on strike. In our body we have many limbs, hands, legs, the mouth, genitals. We have also the belly which actually does not move. All the other limbs are quite active in the sense that they are moving. Like the hand you see, it's moving. My mouth is speaking. And after this class I will have to walk back to the place. So I use my legs. So throughout the day various limbs are active doing different things. My eye is busy accumulating visual stimuli so that I can make sense and act based on what my eyes have captured. So one day they all got together. They had a meeting. In fact a secret meeting, where they said this is not done. It's a great injustice to all of us. We all work the whole day, morning to night. And this stomach just sits there and does nothing. In fact all the hard work what we put in, in fact the hand started saying the food which we pick up and put in our mouth. And the legs said all the hard work we do morning to night, all the walking I do, the stomach sits there, does nothing, and simply enjoys. So they all got together and said it's not done. It's a great injustice. This cannot happen. We will not allow this to happen. And they all decided to go on strike. Why strike? Just like the strikes happen, the workers do strike to teach management a lesson, to bring them on the right path. So here the limbs went on strike so that they can teach a lesson to the stomach. Now you see how you can enjoy if we do not work. Let us see how you will enjoy. So they all went on strike. And the hand said I will not do anything. I will not write anything. I will not pick up anything. The legs said I will not walk. We are not going to put any food in the mouth. If at all we get food we will try to enjoy independently. And all the organs, all the limbs were in agreement. Sometimes you know when there is a union and there is some small section of people who are not in agreement, who don't want to participate in strike. But here it was a clear majority. All of them unanimous decision. Yes we should teach stomach a lesson. And they all went on a strike.
Themes: analogy, body, interdependence
 
URL: https://youtu.be/FXBujCZFnQY?t=688
 
Content-type: story
 
Audience: Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
 
Content: So one day passed. Two days passed. No food was put in the mouth. And the stomach also was nothing there to digest. And all the limbs among themselves were discussing and were quite happy that very soon the stomach is going to learn the lesson. But it so happened that while the food was not being put in the mouth, even all the limbs also started feeling some kind of weakness, some kind of lack of nourishment. They started wondering, what is happening? And then the hand realized, okay maybe we are not getting food. So the hand took up some rasgulla and started squeezing that rasgulla so that all the nutrients, the minerals, the vitamins, whatever is there we get from the food, which is required for the nourishment of our cells, for the nourishment of the body, so that the hand can assimilate that from the food directly. But a time came when the hand even did not have the energy to even squeeze that rasgulla, to pull out some kind of nutrients from it. And all the organs, all the limbs slowly, little by little, little by little became completely weak, could not even move. So they all somehow got together and said, looks like we have done a big blunder. Looks like our understanding of how we are going to get nourished was wrong. And next day they called up the strike. And as usual the food was put in the mouth and all the organs started doing their respective functions. The stomach started digesting the food which was put in the mouth and all the organs started getting their respective nourishment. Now here one may say, is it unfair that all the limbs are working so hard, that they are not getting the nutrients, that they are not getting the nutrients, they are working so hard, but they are not enjoying the food directly. We have never in our life thought that this is unfair. It is very much acceptable fact to us that each part of the body, each limb of the body is working holistically for the whole, for the whole, which is the complete body. And when each limb does its respective function, then as per the design, design of this body, design of this mind body system, different limbs get their respective quota of nourishment.

URL: https://youtu.be/FXBujCZFnQY?t=869
 
Content-type: philosophy
 
Audience: Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
 
Content: Something very similar is the case of how the whole system, our world has been designed by the Lord for us to be happy. It may look that this is not done, this is not fair. But Krishna in the Gita says, we are his amshas. We are his parts. Just like hand is a part of this body. Limbs are the parts of the body. We are his amshas. And amshas the parts are meant to serve the whole. Imagine this hand does not function and there is severe serious infection happening and it stops functioning and that infection starts spreading. Who knows the doctor may say, this hand is no longer useful. In fact we may have to amputate the hand. So likewise, we all are amshas of the Lord and we have been designed to serve the whole. The Lord being the whole. The Lord being the supreme creator. The Lord being the supreme absolute truth. We are his amshas and we have been designed to serve the whole, to give pleasure to the whole. Now somebody may wonder why it is like that. Why the Lord is so mean that he wants to enjoy and he expects us to serve him. Same question we can ask about our limbs. But we never ask that question. It is accepted fact. So long as these are limbs of my body, they are meant to serve the whole. And it is not a master servant relationship where the servant is simply serving the master, the master is simply enjoying and the poor servant is just starving to death. No. When the servant serves the master, he in turn gets his quota of nourishment, his quota of happiness. In fact if you see the servants in houses of rich aristocratic people, they may be cooking very so called elaborate dishes, but I am sure after the master have honored the dishes, they also enjoy the same.

Content: In the case of the Lord, it is slightly different. In fact, the design is such like that we are meant to serve the whole, the Lord, and the Lord in turn is busy with only one thing. He has many many energies to take care of universal affairs. He is simply enjoying the service of his devotees and reciprocating that service by serving his devotees. Sometimes we have an understanding of the Lord, who is the supreme creator, he is the maintainer, he is the annulator, so he must be very very busy. Just like you know, you have sometimes big business men, they are very busy. You take an appointment. You have to struggle for days together. Now we extrapolate that. Imagine the Lord, who is not just managing a particular company, not just a country, not just one particular continent, not just one universe, many many universes, material and spiritual. And the Lord has innumerable energies which is effortlessly managing everything. And what is the Lord doing? Lord is enjoying the company of his devotees who are serving him and in turn he is busy serving his devotees. Just like Arjuna decided to serve the Lord and the Lord in turn served his devotees by taking a menial position of a chariot driver. So when we serve the Lord, just like the limbs serve this body as a whole and they get the required nourishment, likewise when we serve the Lord, when we please the Lord, when we give enjoyment to the Lord, we get our quota of enjoyment. That's why Krishna in the Gita says, bhoktaram yagyatapasam. I am the Bhokta. I am the enjoyer. When the limbs try to enjoy independently without serving the whole, it cannot enjoy. What is lust? Lust means desire to enjoy independent of Lord. Because of lusty desires, because of material desires, we make independent plans of enjoyment and we do not get enjoyment. In fact, because of those plans, we attract miseries in our life. Yehi. Samrsparsha. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. Jaya. For little pleasure which we may get by being enjoyer, we attract lot of miseries in our life. In fact, we attract lot of mess in our lives, lot of anxiety in our lives and the peace of mind which we all are striving for becomes completely elusive. So, bhoktaram yagyatapasam. So, Krishna says, one who understands that I am the enjoyer. So, there's nothing wrong in enjoying life.

Content: Prabhupada was asked, what's the goal of your movement? Prabhupada said, the goal of our movement is to enjoy life. Sometimes people think spirituality is against enjoyment. Spirituality is all about giving up, giving up enjoyment, giving up luxuries, giving up money, giving up wealth, giving up family members, renunciation. We are meant to be blissful. That's what we constitutionally are. But if we introspect, today we are in anxiety. We do not have peace of mind. We are entangled. We are not happy. We are not blissful.

Content: the life was structured in such a way, there was a system called varnashrama, the life was structured in such a way that there was a system of varna and ashrama. There were four Varnas. What are the four Varnas? The four Varnas were Brahmana, Kshatriya, Vaishya and Shudras. And there were four Ashramas. What are the four Ashramas? Dhramachari Ashram, Grahasta Ashram, Vanaprastha Ashram and Sanyasa Ashram. So, everyone in the society was following this Varna Ashrama system situated in his proper Varna and Ashrama. Mind you, when we are talking about four Varnas and Ashramas, we are not talking about so-called caste system. The degraded understanding of caste system is that a son of a Brahmana just by having born in a family of a Brahmana claims himself to be a Brahmana. Bhagavad Gita says that this understanding is wrong. This classification as a Brahmana, Kshatriya, Vaishya or Shudra is not by birth. It is by Guna, Karma, Vibhagasha.

Next 25 years, a person would enter into Grahasta Ashrama. Assuming that human life was 100 years divided into 4 phases, a person would engage in family matters, worldly pleasures but within some boundaries of framework. Not unrestricted, unregulated enjoyment. Because, unrestricted, unregulated enjoyment can lead us tangent from the purpose of the life. Then after 50 years a person understanding the goal of life is self realization, God realization, a person would start voluntarily detaching himself from family life and that stage was called Vanaprastha Ashram.

That's why Krishna sends such representatives, such acharyas like Srila Prabhupada, who come and enlighten us, enlighten the society. In general, make us aware that there is a higher purpose to human form of life. So for us to do justice to this human form of life, we have to regularly aspire for satsang. By the way, what's the meaning of satsang? Satsang means, sat means truth, sang means to associate with. Invariably, we are born in darkness of ignorance. And by satsang, by associating with pure devotees, by reading scriptures like Bhagavad Gita, Bhagavatam, Ramayana, Mahabharata and all that, we can slowly, little by little, dissipate the darkness of ignorance and come to light.

URL: https://youtu.be/oGSs1r_6m6E?t=247
 
Content-type: analogy
 
Audience: Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
 
Content: So before we talk about how to dispel anxiety, let us first spend some moments understanding why we become anxious, what is the root cause of anxiety. There could be many reasons why a person can become anxious, but what is the root cause, the fundamental reason because of which we go through anxiety? Bhagavatam gives a wonderful example of the moon in the sky. When we see the moon in the sky, let's say on a full moon day, the moon is shining brilliantly, and if you see the reflection of that moon in a river, although the moon in the sky is stationary, shining brightly, but if you see the reflection of that very moon, which is stationary in the sky, in a river or in a pond or in some water body, you will see the reflection is quivering. Again, to what extent the reflection quivers, shakes, depends on how much current is there in the water, in that pond, in that river body, or whatever you can call it. If the water is completely stationary, hypothetical case, then even the moon, the reflection is stationary, there is no quiver. But if there is turbulence in the water, if there is waves in the water, then even the moon which is stationary appears to be moving. So likewise, we as amsa of the Lord, as part and parcel of the Lord, we are sat, chit, and ananda. We are blissful. We are not meant to be having anxiety. We are not meant to go through anxious moments in our lives. Our constitutional position is we are always prasannatma, blissful, just like the moon. It's not supposed to quiver, it's not supposed to shake. Even the reflection of that very moon is supposed to be, that moon which is stationary, the reflection also has to be stationary, but depending on the water body in which the moon is reflected, it appears to be moving. So likewise, when we, the spirit soul, pure soul, identify with this body, with the mind, with the senses, this pure soul appears to be quivering, appears to be in anxiety, appears to be going through a lot of tension, stress, worry.

Take any person in this world, be the richest man in this world, there is hankering for something which he does not have, and lamentation falls for something which a person has lost or may lose. Brahmabhoota prasannatma, so that is our nature. So Srimad Bhagavatam says, if one thinks that he has many enemies, he is mistaken. The real enemy is his uncontrolled mind and uncontrolled senses. So when we identify with our body, when we think that I am the body, dehatma buddhi, and my happiness is in enjoying the senses, this material world is created for an opportunity for the soul to gratify his senses, enjoy the senses, be an enjoyer, be the lord, be the controller, be the center of attraction. So when we identify with the body, then the body goes through a lot of transformations: birth, death, old age, diseases, and the soul identified with that body also goes through a lot of worries, a lot of anxieties, a lot of pains, a lot of trials, a lot of tribulations.

So therefore, when we associate. With a pure devotee of the Lord, who is faithful servant of the Lord, who has faith in the Lord, who has conviction in the Lord, who has love for the Lord, that faith also rubs on us.

So it is nothing wrong to be dependent on the Lord. In fact, if we are not dependent on the Lord, it's an artificial position. If it's raining outside, is it wrong? Is it? Will we call a person as a coward if he takes an umbrella to protect himself? So likewise, Krishna in the Gita says. If you become conscious of me, by my grace, you will cross over all obstacles of conditional life. As you surrender, so shall I reciprocate. If you don't want Lord, he doesn't interfere in our lives. If we want him, to the extent, we want him, to the extent, we surrender to him, to that extent, he reciprocates.
